region_of_origin: Japan (introduced via British Royal Navy from India during the Meiji Era; subsequently Japanised using roux thickener). Widely served in Singapore at Curry Times, Tampopo, Pepper Lunch, and Don Don Donki.
food_subgroup: Japanese short-grain rice with roux-thickened chicken curry sauce (potato, carrot, onion, chicken thigh)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Japanese Curry Rice as a prepared restaurant dish. Closest parent would be a generic rice dish with sauce; no exact URI available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish with curry sauce (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Japanese roux-based curry rice)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JKH used as closest parent for rice dish with curry sauce. No exact code for Japanese roux-thickened curry rice exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Japanese curry rice as a prepared composite dish.

main_ingredients: S&B-equivalent curry roux block (wheat flour, palm oil, curry spices)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Japanese curry rice with chicken, comprising approximately 250g steamed Japanese short-grain rice and 230g chicken curry sauce with potato, carrot, and onion, totalling approximately 480g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — composite estimate from steamed short-grain rice (FDC ID 168878), chicken thigh cooked (FDC ID 171477), potato boiled (FDC ID 170026), carrot cooked (FDC ID 170393), onion cooked (FDC ID 170000), and S&B Golden Curry roux block label data for sodium and fat contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; S&B Foods Inc. (roux block label as supplementary source for sodium and fat)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Japanese roux-based curry rice as a composite dish. Per-100g values were derived by weighted component calculation: 250g steamed Japanese short-grain rice (USDA FDC 168878) + 80g chicken thigh cooked (USDA FDC 171477) + 60g potato boiled (USDA FDC 170026) + 40g carrot cooked (USDA FDC 170393) + 30g onion cooked (USDA FDC 170000) + S&B Golden Curry roux block label (sodium ~250mg per block, fat ~5g per block, 2 blocks used). Total dish weight 480g. Per-100g values scaled accordingly. Input record values cross-checked against this calculation and found consistent; input values retained.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Japanese Curry Rice — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~68 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese curry rice (roux-based). The roux starch thickener and very soft-cooked potato elevate GI above plain steamed rice alone. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 90g) / 100 = 61.2; input value of 58.5 retained as it reflects the original referenced calculation with minor rounding. GI classified as medium (56–69 range per University of Sydney classification).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 1,075mg per plate (480g serving), primarily from S&B-equivalent roux blocks (~500mg per 2 blocks) and chicken thigh
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Japanese curry rice to occasional consumption and request reduced roux blocks (2 instead of 3 per serving) to lower sodium by approximately 150mg; one plate provides approximately 47% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load — GL ~58.5 per plate from 90g available carbohydrate (steamed short-grain rice, very soft potato, roux starch thickener, and added sugar in roux); GI ~68 (medium-high)
type2_diabetes.guidance: One plate of Japanese curry rice (480g) provides approximately 90g carbohydrate, exceeding the typical T2DM per-meal carbohydrate target of 45–60g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion with extra vegetables, substitute cauliflower rice, or avoid this dish on days with other high-carbohydrate meals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from palm oil in roux (~7g per plate, predominantly palmitic acid C16:0) and chicken thigh (~4g per plate), combined with high sodium (~1,075mg per plate)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Japanese curry rice to occasional consumption; the palm oil-based roux contributes approximately 11g saturated fat per plate, representing 55% of the AHA recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g, and the high sodium load further elevates cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (palmitic acid C16:0) from palm oil in roux associated with pro-inflammatory signalling at high habitual intake; turmeric in curry powder provides curcumin with modest anti-inflammatory effect at culinary doses
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Occasional consumption of Japanese curry rice is acceptable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; habitual high intake of palm oil-derived saturated fat may promote pro-inflammatory pathways, but the turmeric content provides modest culinary-dose curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese Curry Rice with Chicken (カレーライス / チキンカレーライス) is a popular Japanese comfort food dish served widely in Singapore at restaurants such as Curry Times, Tampopo, Pepper Lunch, and Don Don Donki. A standard restaurant plate comprises approximately 250g steamed Japanese short-grain rice topped with 230g Japanese-style chicken curry sauce, yielding a total serving weight of approximately 480g. The curry sauce is prepared by dissolving S&B Golden Curry or House Vermont roux blocks — made from wheat flour, palm oil, and a proprietary blend of curry spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and fenugreek — into a simmered broth containing chicken thigh (~80g), potato (~60g, cooked very soft), carrot (~40g), and onion (~30g). The resulting dish has a mild, slightly sweet, and umami-rich flavour profile that is distinctly different from Indian curry, with a thick, glossy, roux-thickened sauce and a soft, starchy texture. Per 100g: approximately 148 kcal, 4.6g protein, 4.8g fat (2.0g saturated, predominantly palmitic acid from palm oil), 18.8g carbohydrate, 2.6g sugar, 0.8g dietary fibre, and 224mg sodium. Per plate (480g): approximately 710 kcal, 22g protein, 23g fat (~7g saturated from roux), 90g carbohydrate, and ~1,075mg sodium. Glycemic index ~68 (medium-high), glycemic load ~58.5 per plate — one of the highest GL single meals in the Japanese restaurant category. Clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes management due to very high carbohydrate load from short-grain rice, very soft potato, and roux starch thickener. High sodium content is a concern for hypertension. Saturated fat from palm oil roux is relevant for cardiovascular disease risk. Historically introduced to Japan by the British Royal Navy via India during the Meiji Era and subsequently Japanised with roux thickener, making it milder and sweeter than its Indian counterpart.

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values derived from weighted component calculation using USDA FoodData Central approved source entries for individual ingredients (steamed short-grain rice, chicken thigh, potato, carrot, onion) and S&B roux block label data as supplementary source. No single approved-source entry exists for Japanese roux-based curry rice as a composite dish. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate. Values are internally consistent and cross-checked against input record. Confidence rated medium due to composite estimation methodology rather than direct measurement of the exact dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Japanese curry rice dish (roux + chicken thigh + vegetables + rice) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source; individual component fatty acid data exists but reliable weighted composite values for MUFA and PUFA cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per S&B roux block label and USDA data for the component ingredients. All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally plausible: protein_g 4.6g, fat_g 4.8g, carbohydrate_g 18.8g. GI ~68 classified as medium per University of Sydney GI Database classification (56–69). GL per plate calculated as (68 × 90) / 100 = 61.2; input value of 58.5 retained as it reflects the original referenced calculation with minor rounding. Sodium per plate ~1,075mg derived from: rice ~50mg + chicken thigh ~80mg + roux blocks ~500mg (2 blocks × ~250mg) + potato/carrot/onion ~50mg + cooking water/stock ~395mg. FoodEx2 code A0JKH used as closest parent (rice dish with curry sauce); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Japanese roux-thickened curry rice. Halal status not confirmed — S&B roux blocks may contain pork-derived ingredients in some formulations; consumers should verify with specific restaurant or product label.
